chemical_profile.ideal_formula: SiO2 (with trace Ti, Al, Mn or microscopic inclusions)
chemical_profile.atomic_structure_notes: Trigonal quartz framework. The color mechanism of rose quartz is unusual: unlike most colored quartz varieties (which have point-defect color centers), rose quartz color comes primarily from microscopic inclusions of a pink titanium-bearing mineral (aluminum phosphate hydroxide — dumortierite-related fibers, or possibly bosiite). Some researchers propose color from Al-OH color centers. Rarely forms well-developed crystals (unlike amethyst/citrine); typically massive.
chemical_profile.chemistry_notes: Rose quartz is unusual among quartz varieties in being almost always massive (not crystalline). Well-formed pink quartz crystals (sometimes called 'crystalline rose quartz' or 'pink quartz') may have a different color mechanism (Al-P centers). The fibrous inclusions responsible for asterism in star rose quartz are rutile needles. Color fades on prolonged exposure to direct sunlight.

crystallography.crystallography_notes: Rose quartz is exceptional among colored quartz varieties in rarely forming well-developed crystals. Almost exclusively massive (no crystal faces). Crystal-clear pink 'rose quartz crystals' sold in the gem market are typically pale amethyst or treated material. True rose quartz is massive, waxy to vitreous, with diffuse internal color.

physical_properties.physical_notes: Typically translucent rather than transparent due to fibrous inclusions causing light scattering (milky quality). Rarely transparent. Chatoyancy and asterism possible when properly oriented. Color fades in prolonged direct sunlight.

color_profiles.name: Classic Rose Quartz
color_profiles.cause: Microscopic pink fibrous inclusion of Ti-bearing Al-phosphate hydroxide mineral distributed through massive quartz; optical scattering.

color_profiles.name: Star Rose Quartz
color_profiles.cause: Rutile needle inclusions in three orientations within quartz produce asterism when cut as cabochon.
color_profiles.conditions: Sri Lanka (Maingkha), Brazil — massive material with oriented rutile silk.

color_profiles.name: Deep Rose / Intense Pink
color_profiles.conditions: Some Madagascar and Brazil material; less common.

appearance.visual_notes: Rose quartz has a characteristic soft, diffuse, milky-pink quality from the light-scattering fibrous inclusions. It is never facet-grade transparent in classic form. The color is gentle and warm — not vivid like ruby or pink tourmaline but consistently soft. Star rose quartz cut as cabochon shows the six-rayed star most vividly under a point light source.

optical_and_special_effects.fluorescence: Weak blue or orange-pink under UV
optical_and_special_effects.asterism: Star rose quartz — six-rayed star from oriented rutile inclusions; best shown in Sri Lankan and Brazilian material
optical_and_special_effects.optical_notes: Uniaxial positive. RI = 1.544–1.553. Typically cloudy/translucent due to inclusions causing light scattering — chatoyancy and asterism arise from these same inclusions. Color fades on prolonged UV/sunlight exposure.

notable_localities: Governador Valadares and Araçuaí, Minas Gerais, Brazil (world's largest source)
formation_and_occurrence.occurrence_notes: Rose quartz is primarily a pegmatite mineral, occurring in large massive bodies. Brazil's Minas Gerais is the world's largest and most important source, supplying most of the global gem and carving market. Madagascar produces some of the finer pink material.

historical_uses: Carved into beads and figurines in ancient Egypt and Rome
Facial masks in ancient Egyptian burials (rose quartz)
cultural_associations: Universal love-stone in modern metaphysical tradition
Egyptian and Assyrian legend: Aphrodite/Venus stone
Rose quartz spheres and hearts are the most recognizable crystal-healing object globally
Associated with Aphrodite and Isis in ancient traditions
cultural_and_historical.folklore_notes: Rose quartz has one of the most robust modern metaphysical followings of any mineral. Ancient Assyrian and Roman cosmetic recipes include rose quartz facial rollers — a practice that has seen a massive modern revival. Egyptian legend held that rose quartz was a gift from Aphrodite (via Eros) to humanity — used to prevent aging. The association with love and the heart is essentially universal in modern crystal culture.

interpretive_vibe.vibe_notes: Rose quartz is mineral warmth — not vivid, not sharp, not crystalline. It diffuses its pink into the whole mass and softens light into rose. It is the texture of a gentle thing, the color of trust and care.

practical_notes.handling_notes: Color fades with prolonged direct sunlight. Durable for jewelry and display. Common form as massive carvings is very stable.
practical_notes.care_notes: Store away from direct sunlight for extended periods. Clean with warm water and soft cloth. Ultrasonic safe. Avoid acids (quartz is generally acid-resistant but some surface finishes are not).
treatments_or_synthetics: Dyeing: pale rose quartz sometimes dyed deeper pink
Irradiation: can produce more intense pink
Glass simulants: common in low-end market
Synthetic rose quartz: produced but rare commercially
practical_notes.market_notes: One of the most affordable and widely available gem and display minerals. Dominant in the crystal/wellness market for spheres, eggs, hearts, rollers. Large polished slabs for interior design. Very low cost per kilogram. Star rose quartz (Sri Lanka) commands premium.

prompt_fragments: rose quartz massive — diffuse pink warmth in translucent quartz fog
star rose quartz cabochon — six-rayed asterism in milky blush
Venus stone: Brazil pegmatite pink dispersed through massive silica

provenance.notes: Rose quartz is a variety of quartz. mindat_id 3476 is the rose quartz variety listing. Color mechanism is debated — fibrous inclusion model is current consensus.
